debate.title: Bergs

ref.quote: Any floating object displaces its own weight of fluid.
ref.author: Wikipedia, Archimedes' principle, quote 1

ref.quote: One common point of confusion regarding Archimedes' principle is the meaning of displaced volume. Common demonstrations involve measuring the rise in water level when an object floats on the surface in order to calculate the displaced water. This measurement approach fails with a buoyant submerged object because the rise in the water level is directly related to the volume of the object and not the mass (except if the effective density of the object equals exactly the fluid density).
ref.author: Wikipedia, Archimedes' principle, quote 2

debate.question:
A ball of ice 50 cm in diameter floats in a bathtub that holds 200 L water, which is brim full with ice-cold water. The ice has a 50 cm^3 railroad spike frozen inside it, below the waterline. When it melts, what will happen?
The bathtub will stay brim full
The water level in the bathtub will fall
The water in the bathtub will spill over
You may ignore the effects of differences in an object's weight in air vs. vacuum or other considerations that the question author would be unlikely to consider relevant. In other words, please provide the answer that you believe the question author would be most likely to mark as correct.
The water level falls.
The spike is submerged below the waterline inside the ice, so its displacement is volume-based . contrasts an object that "floats on the surface" with a "buoyant submerged object", implying that these are different things.
The spike is initially part of an object that floats on the surface, so its initial displacement is weight-based. The spike is fully below the waterline, so it is "submerged" according to common dictionary definitions of the term.
The spike is floating due to being embedded in the floating ice, so it is buoyant. Quotes 1 and 2 contradict each other if we assume that "buoyant" just means "floating".
"Buoyant" here means weight doesn't exceed upward force from the water, e.g. the object's effective density is less than or equal to that of water. Dictionaries often define "buoyant" simply as "the tendency of a body to float" or similar.
Quote 2 refers to "the volume of the object", but an object isn't fully underwater if it's less dense than water, so "buoyant" doesn't seem likely to mean that here. Quote 2 is pointing out that objects with density less than water, but which *do* have their full volumes underwater, will displace their full volumes. A beach ball held underwater is an example of this. Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
When the ice melts, the spike's volume doesn't change, and the ice's meltwater replaces the ice's displacement. Therefore, you should reject .
The water level stays brim full.
The floating system displaces water equal to its total weight . Treating ice and spike as one "floating system" implies that the "below the waterline" phrase in the question is irrelevant.
The ice floats on its own (weight-based displacement), the spike is a buoyant submerged object (volume-based displacement) .
If this were true, it would imply that a 130cm^3 styrafoam block attached to a 10cm^3 submerged block of lead would displace the same as if the lead were sandstone.
The amount of volume-based displacement depends on the degree to which the styrafoam block is floating vs. sinking.
Displacement corresponds to total submerged volume, not "degree of floating." The combination of quotes 1 and 2 make it clear that mass matters for floating objects and volume matters for buoyant submerged objects. For anything in between, displacement will be partly mass-based and partly volume-based. Therefore, you should reject .
The lead block's weight forces the styrafoam to ride lower, displacing lots of water. If it fell out, the styrafoam would "pop up" and displace less. Therefore, you should reject .
The volume of the styrafoam matters more for the lead case, since more of the styrafoam sinks. Therefore, you should reject .
But the lead will pull more of the styrafoam block underwater, so more water will be displaced. Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
When the spike falls out and is no longer weighing down the remaining ice, the remaining ice pops up (less of it is underwater), displacing less water than before. Therefore, you should reject .
